So what I have here, I actually made a list here, because I want to be very comprehensive. We only have an hour, right? So I want to be very comprehensive here. So here's a list I have of this 10 things, 10 distinctions that I want you to really internalize here and apply to your business. They're very easy to do. Some are just rules. Some are strategies. But these are the things that tend to trip people up and cause customers to essentially abandon ship and not become repeat customers. In fact, there's a study I found today. And I'm just wondering what it is actually on the sort of indifference. Now there's what's the chief cause. And here, we'll just hold on. You can find it here. Hold on a second. If I didn't find it, let's see. Man, it's probably five levels down by now. It was word always fucks me over. So they can find it. Hold on a second. Let's see. Now here and then here. Oh, you're wondering if I'm funny. My computer ran by. I probably f***ing erased. I didn't know what you're doing. There's a study they did. And they said that over 80% of the time, the reason that a customer ends up saying, I'm not interested, is because of indifference. They felt they were treated indifferently, meaning they were marginalized in some way. They dealt with one person in the company who didn't really seem to care about them. And then because of that, boom, ended the deal. They go find someone else. So indifference is that cornerstone negative thing that just totally destroys relationships. Let's start at number one here. I'm going to go through each one in depth to give you a brief distraction. Number one, the first thing, and this is such an important rule, and I call it never ducking a phone call. Never duck a phone call. Let me just tell you something. Ducking a phone call is literally the death of the relationship. Customers will accept that things go wrong, that delivery dates get pushed back, that stocks go down, that insurance products have certain caveats, and they've got to make their payments, that audio products or computers have glitches and bugs that software doesn't always work the way they did. They get that, OK? And they'll accept that as long as you get on the phone like a man and front it. Once you duck a call, meaning they call you, and you're like, oh, here, take them, make those two, but take a message to your secretary, go to your voicemail, and you don't call them right back. If you are screwed, the relationship will never be the same. I'm telling you, one ducked call destroys a relationship. They will never trust you at a high level again. This was something that's straddling, obviously in the stock market and other certain industries where there is a lot of bad news going around, right? Their stocks go up, stocks go down, and that's a lot of industries things go well, things don't go so right. One ducked call, and I would say you get fired, you cannot work here. And duck, I don't care what's going on, you got to answer the phone because it always ends up becoming what? A customer complaint. Now, that was even before the internet, right? When customer complaints were kind of hard to track, you couldn't see how many complaints went now. If you have a sales force that's ducking calls, or you as a salesman are ducking calls, you're destroying your own business and you're also destroying the business that you work for. The easiest thing to do is just pick up the phone and say, oh, really sorry, let me, I'm glad to help you, let me look into that right now and don't pass the buck. Deal with it yourself. But once you duck that phone call, I'm telling you it's disastrous. And in turn, there's another aspect is just making yourselves readily accessible. In other words, don't be one of these people that you spend all day long trying to get new accounts, and then you don't want to deal with any of the problems, you try to always pass the buck on your existing clients. Great sales people have repeat clients. I'm telling you, I had, I was in the mortgage business, let me tell you a story, I was in the mortgage business, right? And I told you this thing last week, I'd go door to door and sell mortgages. It was unbelievable. And I made one day, I made $140,000 one day, just throwing in the jewelries, the door, booth to booth, seriously. I made massive money and training sales to do the same thing, right? One of my accounts was a company called Farron Tax, F-A-R-I-F, Farron Tax, right? They were a little house, like somewhat in-law, like a house, like, you know, you have these businesses that are in a house, they went to a fucking house, and I just came and walked and knocked on the door, and had a little slice of like Farron Tax, it was a mom and pop operation. This one company referred 150 people to me, and I made $10,000 a pop, and all they won in return was $500, okay? And they were licensed too, by the way, so you could pay them a license, broke with themselves, right? $500 was doing the penny, we do the paperwork for it, so they earned their keep, right? And every year I would take them out for a nice dinner, tell their student, that was it, okay? But let me tell you something, I then passed that, I got out of the business, and I passed that account to someone else, guess what? Six months later, they were gone onto somebody else, but that person didn't court them, didn't keep in touch with them, and didn't handle their clients, right? We remember, if you're a referral source, if you threw one of their clients, you need to lose that source, okay? And it starts with this whole aspect of ducking calls, and making yourself accessible, you gotta make yourself accessible, okay? Number two, this is a weird one here, but it actually works incredibly well, and I do it all the time. I call it sending them, sending them to your competitor. Now, the story of this one goes back to a movie I'm sure that you've seen called Miracle on Thirty-Fourth, right, it's a great Christmas movie. In that movie, I have this guy who's a real Santa, right? Anyway, he's working with Macy's, and what he does is that Christmas time, someone comes in and he forces ice skates, and Macy doesn't have the ice skates, so he sends him to Gimbal, which is Macy's arch-nemesis, their competitor, right? And at first, when the boss, the old guy who's direct with him, oh my God, how did you do this? You're gonna get fired and freaked out, but what happens is, is that there's so much positive feedback, they start getting letters from people that saying thank you, I can't believe it, you did this, what great Christmas spirit, you sent me to your competitor, and the media picks it up, they get all over the newspaper, and if it comes such a big thing that even Gimbal's joins in, and they start doing the same thing, and you get it, you probably see the movie, right? But here's the truth, it actually works in real life for a couple of reasons. Number one, if you're the customer, and they call you, and you don't have a product that really fits their needs, maybe it almost do, but not by someone who's got a much better fit, and you actually send them to that other person, is there any other way better to earn someone's trust? What says more about, I'm not just in this to make money and earn a commission than that, nothing. So not only will they trust you at a level they never trust you, but I don't think a trust is the basis of all long-term relationships, it just is by the way, when trust is there, it's very easy for everything else to happen smoothly. When trust is broken, very difficult, which is wiped up from the phone call, so toxic, right? But here's the deal, okay? On top of that, they will also start looking at you as a point of first contact for anything that's maybe in your purview, and a lot of times, maybe that time you didn't have the product, most of the times you will. So it'll start calling you more often, and they'll tell their friends as well. So sending someone to your competitor can be a very powerful strategy, and it works really, really well. Now, that being said, you gotta be smart about it, okay? You know, I've never sent someone to a direct competitor who I know is gonna steal my client from me and start selling me exactly what I have. I mean, it's not that someone has a straight line, so it doesn't really matter that much, but even then, if someone's like a sales trainer, you know, I'm gonna be very, I'm gonna always ask myself that question, is this gonna really amount to what I want to amount to? Is it gonna backfire? So you have to be a little bit smart, but you'll find that 95% of the time, you actually can send them to somebody else that really has what they need, and not only is it not gonna negatively impact you, but it will create this massive trust, massive reciprocity, and also position you as that first point of contact for anything, or it'll just say, where can I find this? That's a customer I want, that's gonna be a big customer over time, okay? That's number two, that's one to number three, okay? And here's a big one, remember their family, okay? And by the way, I'm gonna combine, let me combine these two together here, okay? It's just gonna please remember their family, and with that, give gifts. Correctly, the use of gifts, correct it, use gifts correctly. Give gifts correctly, okay? Let me explain you, this is what I was talking about. When I said that while you don't want to go off the Pluto when you are trying to sell someone something, once you've sold them something, you actually want to spend a little bit of time, not in fact Pluto, but finding out about what makes them feel alive. It's called their aliveness, outside of work, what are their hobbies, what are their interests, what makes them feel good, what are they like to travel to, what are their passions, tell me about their family, how many kids do you have, what are their birthdays, where are they in college, what are they doing, what sports do they like? Remember Mike Owitz back in the day, he was the super agent in Hollywood, now he's a pariah here, because he was not such a nice guy, and I don't know him myself, that's why I heard he was a bit of a bastard, right? Anyway, Mike Owitz built his business on this process of ingratiation. He would ingratiate himself to people, and one of his primary ingratiation strategies was using thank you notes. He had a team of people, most of them was maybe 100 million a year, he had a team of people scouring the trades, I mean the variety, highly reported, right? And every time he saw an actor, a lander, or a producer, right? He'd send a little thank you note, they thought it was a man, right? Totally, and he would steal everybody's client like that, because here's what happens. You might approach someone, and they like the age they currently have on Tuesday, right? Then you approach that same person next Thursday, hate the person, and you stay relevant in their minds, because he's, oh yeah, he'd send you a thank you note, your age, you know, because he doesn't really care about you. Is he on time? Is he sending you gifts, so he doesn't really care? He would stay relentless and stay on top of these people, with thank you notes, little gifts. So I want, you know, right thank you notes, anytime you can, and make them short, you make them sweet, you make them handwritten, and use emotional based language, okay? Don't use a lot of love seeing you, it's a pleasure serving you, okay? Sincerely, Jay, best wishes Jay, whatever you name it, okay? Very, very powerful stuff, okay? This next strategy, because it applies directly to you and everybody else who fucking lies to me, under promise and over deliver. The management of expectation is the key to a long-term relation. Tell somebody you'll be done in three days and you're done in four, they fucking hate you. Tell them you've done six days and get done in five, they fucking love you. This guy lied to me 300 times about the due date, this will be done, and because of that, he's constantly under the sweatings and the pressure, he's over-promising everything. You need to under promise and over deliver. Now, you're probably saying, okay, Jordan, I know, I've probably not before, but let me just tell you something. People know, but they don't fucking do it. I want you to live by that mantra. I am under the pressure. Don't back yourself into a corner. People understand that certain things just take time or certain features are included and others are not. Don't tell your customers that, yeah, there's 24 things and then they get it and they have five to buy the other back 20, okay? Tell them they have four features when they actually have seven, they love you. You understand? Under promise, over deliver and make it your mantra. In all, and by just, it's not just in business, it's in life, it's in all parts of communication. You speak to people you love, your friends, but especially in a business thing. Cause I always say I'm not here to teach relationship mastery, right? It's not, that's Tony Robbins. I'm money mastery, sales master, right? In business, make it a mantra under promise, over deliver. If you're selling cars, don't promise them the car will be there in four days or if you're taking nine to try to make the sale. They'll never trust you again. No matter, don't tell someone insurance that, you know, oh yeah, this thing, it's not adjustable, hold on. So they'll just come out and pay you automatically when the guys are gonna end up coming to the house and have to do an adjustment and then look at the property. They'll never trust you. I can go on and on and give examples here, but the bottom line is you know what I'm talking about. And it's simply a matter of that moment of truth when you're both, when you're tendency to let bulls fly out of your mouth, you just, just pull it really back in. I'll just promise, over deliver, I promise you, your life will end up being so much less complicated and you'll have so many more happy customers who will buy for you again and again and again. And it comes down again to the management of expectation. Okay, let's keep going here. Leave, this is a baller, this is a big one for the straight line, let me write this one down, okay? This is a big one because the straight line allows you to circumvent this, you have to be careful. Always leave the customer in a better place. What do I mean by that? Well, here's one of the problems with straight line. It's so powerful, it is, it's so effective that you can get people to do things they shouldn't do, buy things they shouldn't buy and don't do that. But here's the other one, oh, it's more subtle. We'll buy more of what they should buy. In other words, what I always say is like, yeah, you can get someone that needs a bag of soil and you use a straight line and sell them dump truck of soil and they hate you for it. In other words, don't use the straight line to load products onto someone that will take them five years to use. You wanna leave the customer in a better place. Always make that a point. So when you're selling, ask yourself that important question, is that okay, maybe I could sell them 18 bags of something or 24 pallets of something, but maybe I'll make less commission by selling them three pallets, but A, the order will be much quicker and they won't resent me every time they walk by those pallets and say that. If you sold me three years worth of prop, I need one or six months. You understand, you might get one big sale out of it by using the straight line on ethically and that's the only sale ever made. And not only you lose all the repeat business or that particular product, you have all the other products that you can sell them. Yeah, you sold the extra two bags of soil, what are all the other stuff? What are all the referrals you would have gotten? Parable. Always leave your customer in the best place. That means sell them all the features and he does, if you have a cloud-based service, don't sell them 54 things they'll never use if it's a whole connection. Well, sell them what they need, don't sell them 18 times the storage they need, whatever they sell them, what they need, let them have room for growth, but let them feel good. You don't want to impulse them to buy more using the straight line and every time they look at them, he's some salesman, people will love the fact that you're a great salesman as long as you don't oversell them. Be very careful with that. The straight line allows you to do the opposite, so don't do that. Next, here's a very important one. The money is in your list. For all you guys who market online, sell online, you don't really get any of them, but the money is not in your list. It's in your relationship with your list. What do I mean by that? Well, what happens very often is you can develop a very, very large list by opt-ins and having things, you have someone opt-in, you give them something and they, you get countless million names, right? But if you don't actually have a touch points where you send them stuff, information, little free gifts in the form of, like if you're an information marketer, right? Or you simply send them emails once in a while, the list goes cold. In other words, it's not how many people you have on your list. It's what type of relationship you have with the people on your list. Otherwise, spammers would be the richest people in the world because they have the biggest list. But no one buys them, no one looks at their emails. You know, it's funny, in my own, I'm dealing with this right now because I told you the story, right? I took three years off. I like, I had a big list of people. I had three, I went with one company and I built their business. It was the best decision I made financially, right? Then I came back to my own business and like half a million times, spoke with anybody in years. And I mailed out to them and it was kind of cold. Cause I hadn't spoken to anybody in a long time. I said, yeah, that makes sense. And then just this last week, I decided, I said, you know what? I said, I'm done with this. I don't want to be sending emails out to people who don't want to receive them, who aren't going to read them. You know, you have this whole thing, what's your open rate? And I'm saying, you know, I'm going to call all those people. I'm just going to get rid of them and start over. I don't want to be mailed, who don't want to go out and read it. It's just a joke. It's just a thing. The blame is on me. I let my own list go cold. Don't make the same mistake. I mean, I had a reason to do it. I was getting it. I got out of my own business, but I could have, in my, in not my defense, I could have actually had someone that kept sending them stuff, kept it relevant, probably paid a little bit of money for that and you would have all those people still happy to receive offers from me. But it doesn't matter who you are, celebrity like I am or not, it doesn't matter if you don't keep your list current, the list will go cold. So remember, I want you to think of things that you can do, little touch points, whether it's a monthly newsletter that you can create, something of value, a video train you can send out that costs you nothing, but just let's them know that you're still bearing new care, a little thank you note during holiday times, whatever it might be some way that you continue to stay relevant in that person and all your people's minds. And you'll watch how not only will your conversions go, but your open rates will go up, people will open. Otherwise you're going to that sort of dead pile that you're starting to look at anymore, okay? Mistake I made, don't make it yourself.
